unit.name: Neural machine translation as sequence-to-sequence learning
unit.description: Introduces end-to-end encoder-decoder NMT with LSTMs and explains why it replaced statistical MT.
unit.summary: The lecture presents neural machine translation as a single end-to-end neural system trained directly for the final task, rather than a hand-factored pipeline [ts=3936s] [ts=3981s]. The model is an LSTM sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder: the encoder reads the source sentence into a representation, and the decoder uses that representation as initial context to generate the target sentence word by word [ts=3966s] [ts=4013s]. Training uses paired source-target sentences, computes prediction losses at each decoder position, averages them, and backpropagates through both decoder and encoder so the whole model is optimized jointly [ts=4120s] [ts=4140s]. The lecture closes by noting that multilayer stacked LSTMs helped these systems, and that neural MT improved quality so dramatically that Google and other companies replaced phrase-based systems within a few years [ts=4394s] [ts=4495s].

key_points.text: Neural machine translation was built as a single end-to-end neural network.
A sequence-to-sequence model uses one LSTM to encode the source sentence and another LSTM to decode the target sentence.
The decoder is conditioned on the encoder's final hidden-state representation and then generates the translation autoregressively.
Training computes losses across target positions and backpropagates through both decoder and encoder networks.
Neural machine translation rapidly displaced statistical MT because quality gains were obvious and large.

unit_summary_for_generation: The lecture presents neural machine translation as a single end-to-end neural system trained directly for the final task, rather than a hand-factored pipeline [ts=3936s] [ts=3981s]. The model is an LSTM sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder: the encoder reads the source sentence into a representation, and the decoder uses that representation as initial context to generate the target sentence word by word [ts=3966s] [ts=4013s]. Training uses paired source-target sentences, computes prediction losses at each decoder position, averages them, and backpropagates through both decoder and encoder so the whole model is optimized jointly [ts=4120s] [ts=4140s]. The lecture closes by noting that multilayer stacked LSTMs helped these systems, and that neural MT improved quality so dramatically that Google and other companies replaced phrase-based systems within a few years [ts=4394s] [ts=4495s].

kp_catalog.name: Neural machine translation with sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder models
kp_catalog.description: Neural MT uses an end-to-end encoder-decoder network that directly models target generation conditioned on a source sentence.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Neural machine translation with sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder models provides prerequisite machinery that later units build on directly.

kp_catalog.name: RNN/LSTM applications and conditional language models
kp_catalog.description: LSTMs can label sequences, encode whole sentences, and generate outputs conditioned on other inputs.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because RNN/LSTM applications and conditional language models captures the core idea that LSTMs can label sequences, encode whole sentences, and generate outputs conditioned on other inputs.

kp_catalog.name: Bidirectional and stacked recurrent networks
kp_catalog.description: Forward-backward encoders and multi-layer recurrent stacks improve contextual representations and model capacity.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because Bidirectional and stacked recurrent networks captures the core idea that Forward-backward encoders and multi-layer recurrent stacks improve contextual representations and model capacity.
